title: Access Keys
text: Access Keys An access key is a character in a widget label or menu item that, when pressed (often in combination with Alt ), gives focus to or activates, the widget or menu item. Access keys help users to navigate Uniface components. Labels and some widgets support access keys. In labels and menus, access keys can be defined by preceding the letter with a percent sign ( % ). In server pages, they can be defined with the accesskey HTML attribute. Access Keys on Microsoft Windows On Microsoft Windows platforms, widgets that accept text input only recognize Alt + keystroke access keys. Access keys are not case-sensitive on Microsoft Windows platforms. Entering Alt E and Alt E has the same effect. The Alt prefix serves to distinguish the access key from other text input. For example, a list box (when it has focus) uses keyboard input as a search profile. It highlights the first list item that matches the keyboard input. If an Alt + keystroke combination is used, the list box knows that the user is entering an access key and, instead of changing the value of the list box, the Alt + keystroke combination acts on the widget associated with that access key. The following widgets do not require an Alt + keystroke combination: Radio group Command button Check box When these widgets have focus, they can be accessed by a single keystroke, without Alt . However, if focus is on a widget that requires an Alt combination, then Alt is required for all access keys, as the widget with focus assumes that keystrokes without Alt are ordinary input. Note: This behavior is specific to Microsoft Windows operating systems, and can vary on other platforms. Consider a form running in Microsoft Windows: Save —a command button using the access key S . When Save has focus: s fires the detail trigger of Save . r or E does nothing. Alt+r gives focus to the Radio Group without selecting or changing a value. Alt+e give focus to the Radio Group and selects the value Eta . Editdb —an edit box using the access key E . When Editdb has focus: r , e , or s inserts those characters into the text field of EDITB. Alt+r changes focus to the radio group Radio . Alt+s changes focus to the command button Save , and fires the detail trigger. Alt+e give focus to the Radio Group and selects the value Eta . Radio —a radio group using the access key R . The ValRep list of Radio defines a , b , c , and E as access keys to items of the radio group. When Radio has focus: R does nothing. a , b , c , or e change the value of Radio . Note: When Radio does not have focus, access keys defined in the ValRep list can only be accessed by typing an Alt + keystroke combination. Alt+a , Alt+b , Alt+c , or Alt+e also change the value of Radio . Alt+S changes focus to command button Save , and fires the detail trigger. Related Links Keyboard Support on Microsoft Windows Many Uniface applications, including the Uniface IDE, run on Microsoft Windows platforms. Uniface provides the MSWINX keyboard translation table(KTT) as default to handle keyboard input on this platform. This translation table follows Windows conventions for navigation, and cut, copy, and paste actions. Reserved Keys on Microsoft Windows It is possible to redefine the behavior of keys by editing the MSWINX keyboard translation table or using your own customized version. Such changes will affect the behavior of Unifields, but may be ignored by widgets. Define Keyboard Shortcuts for Menu Items Menu definitions can define keyboard shortcuts that enable the user to select a menu item using the keyboard rather than the mouse. Keyboard shortcuts can be defined as an access key or an accelerator key. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
